books.title: Everyone Else Seems to Say No So Easily
books.subtitle: For the woman watching other people set limits like it's nothing — and measuring her own swallowed yeses against their easy, guiltless boundaries
books.desc: You watch other people decline things — calmly, in a sentence, without a follow-up apology — and something in you twists. They make it look effortless. You'd have over-explained, hedged, and caved. This is a book for the mind that measures its own boundaries against everyone who seems to have figured it out, and always comes up the weaker one.
books.long_desc:
She said no like it was nothing. One clean sentence, no apology trailing behind it, no three-day guilt hangover. And you sat there doing the math you always do — how does she make it look so easy, why does it cost me so much, what is she made of that I'm not. By the time you finished comparing, you'd already said yes to the thing you didn't want.

This book is for the millennial woman who measures her own limits against everyone who seems to set theirs so cleanly. The coworker who leaves at five. The friend who 'just isn't going to make it.' The woman online with the calm caption about protecting her peace. They all look like they were handed an ease you missed, and every time you watch them, the verdict comes back the same: everyone else seems to say no so easily, and you're the one still folding.

You'll come to recognize how comparison runs your boundary life from the cheap seats. How you study other people's no's for a technique you can copy, and find only proof that you're behind. How 'she just has better boundaries' quietly translates to 'she's better than me.' How you never actually see the cost their boundaries had — only the clean, finished version, edited down to a single confident line — and how you've been grading your messy, real, in-progress self against everyone else's highlight.

We'll sit with the measuring itself. Not to win the comparison, not to make you the one who says no most impressively, but to set the scale down entirely — because a boundary borrowed to look like someone else's never holds. Yours will sound like you: hesitant some days, kind, a little long-winded. That's not a worse boundary. That's a real one.

This book will not turn you into the woman who declines things flawlessly. It won't promise that the comparing stops, or hand you her exact sentence to perform. It will not make your relationships a contest you're finally winning. Nothing here will help you come out ahead, because that was never the point.

What it offers is your own chair, at your own table, and a way to say your own no — in your own words, at your own pace — without first checking how it measured up to hers. You were never behind. You were just looking at the wrong table.

Book 3 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: She said no in one sentence. You'd have written three paragraphs and still said yes.
books.author: Theo Quill
keywords: why is it so hard for me to say no
how to set boundaries like other people
feeling like everyone has it together but you

books.title: The Yes That Arrives Before You Do
books.subtitle: For the woman whose mouth says 'of course' while her whole body braces — learning to trust the alarm that fires a half-second before you agree to one more thing
books.desc: There's a flinch that happens a half-second before you say yes — a small bracing in the chest you've trained yourself to talk over. This is a book about learning to trust that flinch instead of overriding it. Because the alarm isn't being dramatic. It's telling you the truth a beat before your mouth does.
books.long_desc:
Someone asks you for something, and you feel it before you decide anything — a tightening, a held breath, a quiet 'oh no' somewhere under your ribs. And then you hear your own voice say 'of course, happy to.' The yes arrives before you do. By the time the rest of you catches up, you've already agreed, already absorbed it, already promised away an evening you needed.

This book is for the millennial woman who keeps a flawless face in meetings and across dinner tables while her body sets off an alarm no one else can hear. You're not anxious for no reason. You've just learned to treat your own internal warning as static — something to push through, apologize for, override with a smile — instead of information arriving exactly on time.

You'll come to recognize how the false alarm of boundary-fear actually works on you. How the dread spikes before the conversation has even happened, so you say yes just to make the spike stop. How 'I'll just handle it' is the sound of an alarm being silenced rather than answered. How the people you fear disappointing are rarely the ones you've actually heard say a hard word — and how the fear is usually rehearsing a fallout that has never once come.

We'll sit with the moment between the ask and the answer. Not to script you, not to send you in braced for a fight, but to slow that half-second down enough that you can hear what your body already knows. The flinch isn't fragility. It's the most honest part of the exchange.

This book will not hand you a phrase to memorize or teach you to go cold to stay safe. It won't promise that the bracing disappears, or that the right words make the fear of being 'too much' evaporate for good. There is no technique here that turns the people you love into problems to manage. The work is quieter than that, and kinder.

What it offers instead is a chair pulled out, and a way to stay seated in your own limits while you say the true thing across the table — gently, plainly, without rehearsing the disaster first. You are allowed to trust the part of you that knew before you spoke.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: Your 'sure, no problem' got there before you did.
books.author: Theo Quill
keywords: how to set boundaries without feeling guilty
stop saying yes when you mean no
how to stop being a people pleaser
setting boundaries at work and home

books.title: The Good One Doesn't Need Anything
books.subtitle: For the woman who learned that having needs makes her difficult — and has been quietly overgiving to prove she's easy to love
books.desc: Somewhere along the way you came to believe that a boundary is just a need wearing a coat — and that needs are what make a person hard to love. So you became the easy one, the low-maintenance one, the one who never asks. This is a book about the buried belief underneath all that overgiving, and what it costs to keep proving you don't need a thing.
books.long_desc:
You don't think of it as shame. You think of it as being considerate. Being the easy one. The friend who never makes it weird, the colleague who absorbs the extra, the daughter who doesn't add to anyone's plate. But underneath the considerateness is a quieter belief you've carried so long it feels like personality: that having a need is a flaw, that asking is a burden, that a boundary is just selfishness with better branding — and that if people saw how much you actually wanted, they'd love you less.

This book is for the millennial woman who overgives in every relationship to cover a defect she's sure is hers. You set no limits not because you have none, but because protecting them would mean admitting you matter enough to protect — and some old voice says you don't. So you give and give, and call the resentment that builds up 'just being tired.'

You'll come to recognize how shame runs the whole thing from underneath. How 'I don't want to be difficult' is the thing you say right before you abandon yourself. How the apology arrives before the request, softening it into nothing. How being needed feels safe and having needs feels dangerous — and how that single asymmetry has quietly organized your whole life.

We'll sit with the belief itself, the buried one: that something about you is too much, and that the job of every relationship is to keep it hidden. Not to argue you out of it with affirmations, but to look at it plainly, in good light, across the table — which is the one place shame has never been able to survive.

This book will not tell you to 'value yourself' and call it solved. It won't promise that the old voice goes silent, or hand you a confidence that erases the years you spent believing you were the problem. It will not turn your relationships into ledgers of what you're owed. There is no fixing you here, because you were never the thing that needed fixing.

What it offers is a chair that was always meant for you, and a way to say 'this is what I need' across the table without first apologizing for the fact that you breathe. The good one was allowed to need things all along.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: You decided long ago that needing things was the thing to hide.
books.author: Theo Quill
keywords: how to stop people pleasing and overgiving
feeling like a burden self help
learning to have needs in relationships
how to ask for what you need

books.title: I Used to Feel This More
books.subtitle: For the caregiver mourning the warmth that used to come easily — when the part of you that felt everything has gone quiet, and you miss her
books.desc: No one warns you that compassion fatigue feels like a loss. You remember when this work moved you, when you came home full instead of hollow. This is the book for the quiet grief of a heart that has gone numb from giving — for missing the person you used to be. It will not rush you back to feeling. It will sit with you in what is gone, and help you understand that the numbness is not the end of your tenderness, only the cost of it.
books.long_desc:
There was a version of you who felt this more. She cried at the good outcomes and the hard ones. She came home from a long day moved by what she had witnessed. She had room in her chest for the people she loved at the end of the night, not just at the start. You remember her. And somewhere along the way, without a funeral or a date you could point to, she went quiet — and you have been quietly grieving her ever since.

This book is for you: the woman who gives care for a living and cannot understand why she feels so little now. The nurse who has stopped letting it in. The therapist who watches herself going through the motions. The one whose family says she seems far away, and is not wrong. You are not cold. You are not broken. You are mourning a tenderness that has been spent down to nothing, and grief is exactly the right word for it.

Pull up a chair. We are going to talk about the losses that compassion fatigue quietly stacks up — the loss of feeling, the loss of the people and moments that used to land, the loss of who you were before the caring outpaced the refilling. We will let it be sad. We will not pretend the numbness is fine or productive or a sign you have finally gotten tough. We will name what was taken and, just as honestly, what you may need to set down on purpose so you can stop carrying it.

Nothing here will hurry you back to your old self or promise that the warmth returns on a schedule. There is no exercise that resurrects what is gone or shames you for not feeling enough. This is not a plan to optimize your way back to caring. It is a conversation about loss — and about the strange relief of admitting that something in you died a little, so the rest of you can finally be tended.

You are allowed to grieve who you used to be. You are allowed to miss her at the same table where you are learning to be kind to who you are now. The feeling is not gone forever. It is resting, the way you should have been allowed to all along.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You did not stop caring. You are grieving the version of you who could.
books.author: Ravi Santos

books.title: The Day Everyone Needs You at Once
books.subtitle: For the woman who cares for a living and has nothing left to pour — when every person at the table is reaching for you and there is no more room
books.desc: There is a particular kind of tired that no weekend fixes: the tired of being the one everyone reaches for. This is the book for the day when the messages, the meetings, the people you love, and the people you are paid to help all arrive at the same moment and your cup is already empty. It will not hand you a better system for giving more. It will pull up a chair and help you understand why there is no room left, and what it means to finally make some.
books.long_desc:
It is the kind of day where everyone needs you at once. The patient, the client, the kid, the team, the text that just lit up your phone again. You answered the first ten. You meant to get to the rest. And somewhere around the middle of the afternoon you stopped feeling anything at all — not because you stopped caring, but because the caring had nowhere left to go.

This book is for you: the nurse, the therapist, the teacher, the manager, the one your family calls first. The woman who is good at holding everyone, and who has quietly run out of room. You are not too sensitive and you are not failing at your job. Your cup is empty because you have been pouring from it without anyone pouring back, and the flood of need does not pause to let you refill.

Pull up a chair. We are going to sit with the overwhelm of compassion fatigue the way you would sit across a table from a friend who finally admitted she is drowning. We will name what actually happens when too much arrives at once — the numbness that feels like indifference, the snap of irritation at the people you love most, the dread of one more person needing one more thing. We will look honestly at why you keep saying yes, and what the yes is costing the part of you that needed someone to ask how you are doing.

Nothing here will teach you to absorb more. There is no schedule that makes an empty cup full, no technique that lets you give from nothing. This is not a manual for becoming a more efficient caregiver. It is a conversation about the difference between a heart that is closed and a heart that is simply, honestly, out — and about what it looks like to set the cup down before it cracks, and to let someone sit across from you and tend to you for a change.

You are allowed to not have enough today. You are allowed to be a person at the table and not only the one who serves it. The people who love you would rather have you here and human than everywhere and empty. Take the seat. This one is yours.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You can love them all and still have nothing left to give today.
books.author: Ravi Santos

books.title: Still Bracing for the Next Person Who Needs You
books.subtitle: For the helper who never clocks out inside — when your mind keeps scanning for whose crisis comes next, even at your own kitchen table
books.desc: You left work, but you never really left. Some part of you is always scanning — who is about to need something, what is about to go wrong, who you have not checked on. This is the book for the caregiver whose mind never goes off duty, even at her own table. It will not give you a sharper way to monitor everyone. It will help you understand why you cannot put the watching down, and what it would feel like to finally be off call in your own life.
books.long_desc:
You are home. Dinner is on the table, the door is closed, no one has paged you in hours. And still some part of you is up in the watchtower, scanning. Who hasn't texted back. Who seemed off today. What might go wrong tomorrow. Whose crisis is loading next. You are sitting at your own kitchen table and a quieter, tired part of you is still standing watch, the way it has learned it always must.

This book is for you: the woman whose care for others turned into a kind of permanent surveillance. The nurse who reads every silence as a warning. The therapist carrying the caseload home in her chest. The manager, the friend, the daughter who cannot stop monitoring whether everyone around her is okay, because so much has felt like it was hers to catch. You are not paranoid and you are not controlling. You are exhausted from a vigilance that compassion fatigue installed and never turned off.

Pull up a chair. We are going to look honestly at the watcher — the part of you that never clocks out, that scans the people you love for the next emergency, that mistakes hypervigilance for love. We will trace where she came from and why she will not sit down. We will talk about the toll of being on call inside your own life: the way you cannot be present at the table because you are bracing for what comes after it.

Nothing here will sharpen your radar or help you catch more before it falls. There is no system for monitoring everyone more efficiently, no trick to keep more plates from dropping. This is not a manual for becoming an even better early-warning system for everyone else's pain. It is a conversation about what it would mean to come down from the tower — to let the watch end, and to find out that the people you love can be okay without you scanning the horizon for them.

You are allowed to be off duty. You are allowed to sit at the table without one ear on the next crisis. The world will not collapse because you finally stopped watching it for everyone. Take the chair. Let the watch end here.

Book 3 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You came home hours ago. Your mind is still on call.
books.author: Ravi Santos
keywords: cant turn my brain off after work

books.title: The Chair Across From You Is Empty Now
books.subtitle: For the woman grieving a relationship she can't fix — learning to let go of someone without losing herself, one honest conversation at a time
books.desc: Some relationships don't end in a fight. They end in a slow, quiet emptying — until one day the chair across the table is just a chair. This is a book for the professional woman who can manage a crisis at work but can't talk herself out of missing someone, and who is finally ready to grieve instead of fix.
books.long_desc:
You keep setting the table for two.

Not on purpose. It's just muscle memory — the extra cup, the half-turn to share something funny, the conversation you start in your head before you remember there's no one on the other end of it anymore. You're good at endings on paper. You can close a deal, close a quarter, close a door. But this ending didn't sign anything. It just left an empty chair across from you and a place still set, and you don't know how to clear it.

This book is for the woman who is competent everywhere except here. You hold it together all day — the calls, the deadlines, the people who lean on you — and then you come home to a silence shaped exactly like someone who used to fill it. Maybe the relationship ended. Maybe it's still technically here but the person you loved already left it. Either way, something is gone, and the world keeps handing you checklists and silver linings when what you actually lost was a future you could see.

We're going to sit with that instead.

You'll come to recognize the shape of your own grief — the way it arrives as loss, the thing that was taken from you and the thing you have to choose to release, which are not the same and shouldn't be confused. You'll see how loving something has quietly come to feel like an obligation to keep it, and how letting go is not the same as not caring. You'll notice the conversations you keep rehearsing — the apology you're owed, the explanation you'll never get — and what it costs to keep that chair set for a guest who isn't coming. And you'll learn, slowly, how to clear the place at the table without pretending it was never laid.

This book will not give you closure. There's no script here to win the breakup, no five steps to get over it by Friday, no reframe that turns the loss into a lesson and ties it off. It won't tell you the ending was for the best. It won't optimize your heartbreak. It will only pull up the second chair, sit down across from you, and stay while you say the true thing out loud — that it mattered, that it's gone, and that you're allowed to be undone by that for as long as you need to be.

You don't have to be fine yet. You don't have to be grateful for what this taught you. You're allowed to just miss it.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press grief series.
books.angle: You can love a thing and still have to let it go.
books.author: Kira Raman
keywords: grief after breakup books for women
how to move on after a relationship ends

books.title: Watching for the Goodbye Before It Comes
books.subtitle: For the woman who scans every silence for the next loss — quieting the part of you that's already grieving a relationship that hasn't ended yet
books.desc: When you've lost someone before, your mind learns to watch — re-reading the message, decoding the pause, bracing for the leaving you're sure is coming. This is a book for the professional woman whose love has quietly turned into surveillance, and who is exhausted from grieving a person who is still sitting right across from her.
books.long_desc:
You read the text again.

Not because the words changed, but because somewhere between this morning and now they got shorter, and your mind has already built the whole ending out of one missing exclamation point. You watch the pauses. You catalog the tone. You sit across the table from someone you love and instead of being with them, you're scanning them — for the flinch, the distance, the early sign of the goodbye you're certain is on its way. You're not paranoid. You've lost people before. Your mind just learned that if you watch closely enough, maybe next time it won't blindside you.

This book is for the woman whose grief showed up early and never sat down. The hypervigilant one. The one who can read a room at work in three seconds and reads her own relationship the same way — reading every pause for the leaving, rehearsing the loss, mourning a person who is still here and still hers. You think you're protecting yourself. What you're actually doing is grieving something that hasn't happened, every single day, and missing the person in front of you while you brace for their absence.

We're going to put the watching down.

You'll come to recognize the shape of this particular grief — how loss that already happened to you got rewired into a lookout post, how the watcher mind dresses up as love and care when it's really fear wearing their face. You'll see what you're actually scanning for, and how the searching itself creates the distance you're so afraid of finding. You'll notice the conversations you have alone in your head at 2am — the ones where they leave, where you say the perfect thing, where you survive it in advance — and what it costs to keep auditioning for a heartbreak that may never come. And you'll learn, slowly, what it feels like to sit in the chair across from someone without standing guard.

This book will not make you certain they'll stay. There's no method here to read minds, no system to predict the ending, no checklist that earns you a guarantee. It won't tell you to stop caring. It won't hand you hypervigilance dressed up as 'paying attention.' It will only pull up the second chair, sit down where the lookout used to be, and keep you company while you let the watch finally go quiet — long enough to notice that the goodbye you've been bracing for hasn't come, and the person you've been grieving is still right here.

You don't have to keep guarding the door. You're allowed to be in the room. You're allowed to have them while you have them.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press grief series.
books.angle: You've been saying goodbye to them for months. They're still here.
books.author: Kira Raman
keywords: fear of losing someone you love
how to stop expecting the worst in relationships

books.title: The Parents Who Make It Look Easy
books.subtitle: For the parent doing the math at school pickup — measuring their frayed Tuesday against everyone who seems to say no without apologizing
books.desc: There's always another parent who seems to manage it — who says no gracefully, whose family runs smooth, who never looks like they're holding their life together with both hands. You measure yourself against them and lose every time. This is a book for the mind that compares, and for the quiet certainty that everyone else figured out something you missed.
books.long_desc:
You see them everywhere. The parent at pickup who somehow looks rested. The colleague who declines the extra project without a flicker of guilt. The family whose evenings seem calm, whose kids seem regulated, whose 'no' seems to cost them nothing at all. And you stand there with your overflowing calendar and your frayed nerves, doing the math you always do — and coming up short. Again. As if everyone else got a manual for this and yours never came.

This is a book for the working parent whose mind never stops measuring. You compare your behind-the-scenes to everyone else's highlight reel: their easy boundaries against your overcommitted ones, their composure against your end-of-day fray. You scroll, and the comparison sharpens. You watch other parents protect their time so cleanly that it makes your own scattered yeses feel like a personal failing — proof that they understand something about limits, about balance, about life, that you somehow never learned.

Here, you'll come to recognize the comparison for the trap it is. You'll see how it feeds on the parts you can't see — the messes behind their tidy 'no,' the costs hidden under their composure. You'll notice how you only ever measure yourself against the version of someone built to be seen, and how that contest is rigged from the first move. You'll feel the difference between learning from another parent and disappearing into the gap between you and them.

This book will not help you win. It won't teach you to become the parent at pickup who looks effortless, or to out-boundary the people you envy. There is no plan here for finally measuring up, no shortcut to the calm life you keep assuming everyone else is living. Nothing here will close the gap — and that, it turns out, is the point. The gap was never the measure of you.

What you'll find instead is a chair set down where the comparing stops. A quieter possibility: that your no doesn't have to look like anyone else's to be real. That the smoothest-looking family at the table is also just two tired people doing their best. That you were never actually behind — you were only ever looking sideways, when the work was in front of you the whole time.

You don't have to win the comparison. You get to set it down, and finally look at your own table.

Book 3 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: Everyone else seems to say no without apologizing.
books.author: Theo Quill
keywords: comparison and self esteem for parents
stop comparing yourself to other parents
feeling like a failure as a parent
not measuring up as a mom

books.title: The No You Swallow
books.subtitle: When a small 'no' to your boss, your mother, or the class group chat feels like detonating the whole room — a working parent's guide to the false alarm
books.desc: Long before anyone has asked you for anything, your body already knows the answer it's about to give: yes. Yes to the extra shift, the bake sale, the favor you can't afford. This is a book for the parent whose alarm goes off the instant a 'no' comes into view, and who is ready to learn that the danger they're bracing for usually isn't there.
books.long_desc:
It happens before you've even decided. The text arrives, the ask lands, and something in your chest pulls tight. Your jaw sets. You're already rehearsing the apology, already saying yes — not because you want to, but because the no caught in your throat feels like a live wire. Like if you let it out, something would break. The room. The relationship. The fragile peace you've spent all week holding together.

This is a book for the working parent whose alarm goes off first and asks questions later. You know the feeling. Someone needs a thing, and before a single word is spoken your whole body braces — as if you were about to do something dangerous instead of simply protecting forty minutes, one evening, a sliver of yourself. You've learned to read 'no' as a threat. And so you swallow it, again, and call the swallowing kindness.

Here, you'll come to recognize that bracing for what it is: a false alarm. You'll see how the dread of the conversation is almost always bigger than the conversation. How your body sounds the warning for a confrontation that, more often than not, simply doesn't come — the boss who says 'of course,' the friend who understands, the child who is fine. You'll notice the difference between a real rupture and the phantom one you keep predicting at the table.

This isn't a book of scripts. It won't hand you a clever line to say across the kitchen. It won't teach you to manage people or 'win' the moment. There is no five-step system here for becoming someone who never flinches. The flinch is human. We're not going to talk you out of it.

What you'll find instead is a quieter thing: the space between the alarm and the answer. The half-second where you get to notice the bracing, breathe, and choose — not from fear, but from the truth. A no can be soft. It can be kind. It can be said plainly across the table and leave both chairs still standing.

You are not difficult for having a limit. You are not breaking anything by naming it. The danger you keep bracing for has, most of the time, already passed you by.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: Your body braces for a fight that was never coming.
books.author: Theo Quill
keywords: how to say no without guilt

books.title: A Good Parent Doesn't Need a No
books.subtitle: For the mother or father who works twice as hard to hide one quiet belief — that needing a limit at all means something is wrong with them
books.desc: Somewhere along the way you absorbed a quiet rule: a good parent, a good partner, a good employee doesn't need anything. So you keep your limits hidden and work twice as hard to prove you don't have any. This is a book for the person who carries their own needs like a defect, and is ready to set the secret down.
books.long_desc:
You don't say it out loud, but you believe it. That a truly good parent wouldn't need a break. That a truly devoted partner wouldn't keep track of who's more tired. That if you were just a little stronger, a little less needy, a little more capable, you wouldn't have to ask for anything at all. So you don't ask. You absorb. You take the early mornings and the late nights and the invisible second shift, and you tell yourself this is just what love looks like.

This is a book for the working parent who carries their own needs like something shameful — a flaw to be covered, not a fact to be honored. You're not afraid of the conversation because you might lose it. You're afraid of it because asking for a limit feels like a confession: that you're not endless, not selfless, not the bottomless well everyone seems to assume you are. And so you overwork. You over-give. You stay quiet about the thing you need most, because needing it at all feels like proof that something in you is broken.

Here, you'll come to recognize that belief for what it is — not a truth about your character, but an old, inherited rule you never agreed to. You'll see how shame keeps the boundary buried: how it whispers that a real mother, a real father, a real provider wouldn't have to say no. You'll watch the way you hide your limits even from the people who love you, performing a tirelessness no human actually has. And you'll begin to feel the difference between being selfless and being erased.

This book will not fix you, because you are not broken. It offers no method for needing less, no plan for becoming a more tireless version of yourself. It will not teach you to whittle yourself down into someone who never reaches their edge. The edge is real. Everyone has one. Yours is not a defect.

What it offers instead is a chair pulled up beside you, and a quieter possibility: that you could name a need at the table without apology. That you could say 'this is mine to keep' and discover the room doesn't fall in. That the people worth keeping will not love you less for being a person.

You were never supposed to disappear into your own kindness. Wanting a limit doesn't make you selfish. It makes you someone who finally believes they're allowed to be here too.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press boundaries series.
books.angle: You hid the limit because you thought it meant something was wrong with you.
books.author: Theo Quill

books.title: The Patient Parent the Exhaustion Took
books.subtitle: For the depleted parent grieving the warm, present self that compassion fatigue quietly wore away
books.desc: There's a version of you that you miss — the patient one, the one who had warmth to spare. This is a book for the working parent grieving who they were before the well ran dry, and aching for the family they meant to be more present for. It won't tell you to get that person back. It sits with you in the loss and helps you let it be real.
books.long_desc:
No one warned you that you could grieve someone who is still alive — yourself. The parent you meant to be is still in there somewhere: patient at bedtime, unhurried at the table, with a soft kind of attention you used to give freely. Lately you catch only glimpses, and the gap between who you wanted to be for them and who you have the capacity to be lands like a small, daily loss.

This book is for the working parent carrying that quiet grief. The one who loves their kids and their partner no less than before, but who has watched compassion fatigue slowly hollow out the warmth, until the affection is still true but the feeling of it has gone faint. If you have looked at your child across the room and missed them while they were right there, you already understand. This was written for you.

You'll come to recognize what you've actually been mourning. Not your love — that never left — but the surplus you used to have, the easy patience, the version of family life you pictured before the years of pouring out. You'll see how grief and compassion fatigue braid together: how giving care without end is its own slow loss, and how pretending you haven't lost anything keeps the ache from ever softening. You'll learn the difference between letting go of who you were and abandoning who you are.

This book will not promise to give you that person back, or optimize you into a more present parent by Tuesday. It hands you no system, no five-step reset, no script to perform across the table. It will never suggest that you failed, or that your family is a problem to be fixed. There is no recovery plan here, because grief is not a malfunction. It's love with nowhere to put itself.

What there is, instead, is a chair pulled close, and someone willing to say the true and tender thing out loud: that it is okay to miss who you were. That mourning the parent you thought you'd be does not make you ungrateful for the one you are. That you can set down the impossible version and still love the real, tired, deeply human one — the one your kids actually have, who is still showing up.

You are allowed to grieve. And you are allowed to still be here, loving them anyway.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You didn't lose your love. You lost the room you used to feel it in.
books.author: Ravi Santos
keywords: grieving who I used to be

books.title: There's No Room Left For Me
books.subtitle: For the working parent who pours out all day and comes home to find the well already empty — when caring for everyone has filled every inch and left no space to feel anything at all
books.desc: By the time you sit down across from the people you love, there is nothing left in the cup. This is a book for the working parent flooded by everyone's needs at once, who can't find a single quiet inch to hear themselves think. It won't add one more thing to your list — it pulls up a chair and lets you set the whole load down for a while.
books.long_desc:
It usually hits in the doorway. You've been pouring all day — into the inbox, the carpool, the small hands that needed you, the meeting that ran long — and now you're stepping into your own home and someone needs something else, and a quiet panic rises that you cannot name. There is no room left. Not on the calendar, not in your head, not in the part of you that used to have something to give the people right in front of you.

This book is for the working parent who is not lazy and not failing, but flooded. The one who loves their family and still flinches when another need arrives, because the need lands on a person who has already been emptied. If you have sat at your own dinner table feeling far away from everyone you'd die for, this was written for you.

You'll come to recognize the shape of it. How compassion fatigue isn't a lack of love but the cost of love spent without refill. How overwhelm makes everything urgent and nothing reachable, so you stand in the middle of your life unable to pick up any single piece of it. How the numbness you're so ashamed of is the body doing the only thing it can when too much arrives at once — it stops letting more in. You'll see why 'just be present' feels like a slap, and why the answer was never to care harder.

This book will not hand you a system. It will not optimize your mornings or hand you a script for the conversation across the table. It makes no promise that you'll get more done, and it will never once suggest that you, or your marriage, or your tired and beautiful kids, are a problem to be managed. There is no fix here, because you are not broken.

What there is, instead, is a second chair. A place to set the whole armful down and admit, without anyone keeping score, that you have been running on empty for longer than you've let on. Permission to be a person again before you are a provider — to take up a little of the room you've spent years giving away.

You are allowed to be the one who needs something, too. Even now. Especially now.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You gave everyone a piece of you. There was none left over for the love.
books.author: Ravi Santos

books.title: Always Listening For the Next Need
books.subtitle: For the working parent whose mind never clocks off — forever scanning for the cry, the spill, the thing about to go wrong, so braced to catch everyone that they can't feel their own life happening
books.desc: Some part of you is always on duty — one ear cocked for the cry down the hall, the mood at the table, the thing about to go wrong. This is a book for the working parent whose mind never stands down, so braced to catch everyone that they've gone numb to their own life. It won't ask you to care less. It helps you set the watch down, just for a while.
books.long_desc:
You can't remember the last time you were fully off duty. Even now, reading this, part of you is half-listening — for the cough down the hall, the shift in your partner's voice, the small disaster forming just out of sight. You scan faces at the dinner table for the mood before it lands. You brace for the need before it arrives. And somewhere under all that vigilance, you've stopped being able to feel your own life, because every part of your attention is turned toward everyone else.

This book is for the working parent whose mind never clocks off. The one whose love took the shape of constant watching — anticipating, monitoring, catching things before they fall — until the watching became a low hum that never stops, even in the rare quiet. If you have lain awake scanning for a problem that wasn't there, or sat beside the people you love while your attention paced the perimeter, this was written for you.

You'll come to recognize the shape of it underneath. How compassion fatigue turns devoted attention into hypervigilance, so that caring stops feeling like love and starts feeling like a shift that never ends. How the watching mind convinces you that if you stop scanning, something will be missed and it will be your fault. How being permanently braced for the next need leaves no version of you that gets to simply be in the room. You'll see why you flinch at stillness, and why standing down feels less like rest than like dropping your guard.

This book will not teach you to keep watch more efficiently. It hands you no system, no tightened routine, no checklist for staying one step ahead of your family. It will never suggest that the answer is to care less, or that your kids and your partner are threats to be managed. There is no clever trick here, because your vigilance was never a problem to be fixed. It was love that forgot it was allowed to rest.

What there is, instead, is a chair where nothing is about to go wrong, and someone sitting across from you who isn't asking you to catch anything. A place to set the watch down — not forever, not recklessly, just long enough to remember that you are a person in this family, not only the one who keeps everyone safe. To let your attention come home to you for a few minutes and find that the people you love are still fine, and so, for now, are you.

You are allowed to stop listening for the next need. The world will hold for a while. So can you.

Book 3 of the Two Chairs Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You're so busy listening for them that you can't hear yourself anymore.
books.author: Ravi Santos

books.title: The Chair They Used to Sit In
books.subtitle: For the working parent grieving in the cracks of a full life — learning to let go of what loss took, without letting go of who you lost
books.desc: There's a chair at your table that used to be theirs, and most days you set it without thinking. This is a book for the parent who has been carrying a loss between school drop-offs and bedtimes, with no quiet hour to actually feel it. It won't ask you to move on — only to finally sit down.
books.long_desc:
Somewhere in your house there is a chair they used to sit in. Maybe it's a literal one. Maybe it's the side of the bed, the seat in the car, the spot on the couch your eyes still go to. You have a whole life arranged around the place where someone used to be, and you keep that life running — lunches packed, forms signed, small faces fed — while the loss waits in the next room for a quiet hour that never quite comes.

This book is for the working parent who is grieving in the margins. For you, who lost someone and then went back to work on Monday because the world doesn't pause for sorrow and neither do your kids. You have learned to feel it in the car between meetings, to fold it small enough to fit between a tantrum and a deadline, to answer 'I'm fine' so the people who depend on you don't have to carry it too. You are not okay, and you have been so capable about it that no one has thought to ask.

What you'll come to recognize here is that letting go was never the thing grief was asking of you. Not the way they meant it — not closure, not moving on, not making peace and putting them down. There is a difference between releasing what the loss took from you — the future you planned, the phone call you can't make, the version of your life that had them in it — and releasing the person themselves. You can set down the first without ever setting down the second. You can let go of what is gone and keep who is gone. This book is about learning, slowly, which is which.

We'll sit with the guilt of the days you don't think of them, and the ambush of the days you can't stop. We'll make room for the strange arithmetic of grieving while raising children — how you grieve in fragments because that's all the time you're given, and how those fragments still count.

Nothing here will help you get over it. There is no plan, no stage to graduate from, no morning you wake up finished. That isn't the goal, and pretending otherwise would be its own small betrayal. What this book offers instead is the other chair — pulled up, facing yours, and the unhurried company of someone who won't flinch when you finally say it out loud.

Sit down. The chair has been waiting, and so have you. You're allowed to still be grieving. You're allowed to still be here.

Book 1 of the Two Chairs Press grief series.
books.angle: You don't have to be done grieving to be a good parent tonight.
books.author: Kira Raman
keywords: coping with the death of a loved one
how to deal with grief and loss

books.title: Counting the Heads at the Table
books.subtitle: For the grieving parent whose mind never stops scanning for the next loss — when love and fear have started to feel like the same thing
books.desc: Since the loss, you check on everyone. You count the heads at the table, listen for breathing down the hall, and read every cough like a forecast. This is a book for the grieving parent whose love turned into a watch that never ends — and for the part of you that's exhausted from standing guard.
books.long_desc:
You count them now. At dinner, at the door, in the rearview mirror — a quick, automatic headcount you don't even decide to do. Since the loss, some part of you has been standing at the window of your own life, scanning the road for the next thing to take someone. You text to make sure they landed. You crack the bedroom door at night to hear them breathing. You hear a cough and your mind has already written the worst version of the week. You call it being careful. Mostly it's that grief taught you that people can actually be gone, and now you can't unknow it.

This book is for the working parent whose grief became vigilance. For you, who learned the hard way that the floor can drop, and who has been bracing against the next drop ever since — at work, in traffic, in the dark at 2am with your thumb hovering over a text you're embarrassed to send. The people you love have become a list you keep checking. You are tired in a way sleep doesn't touch, because the watch never really ends. No one sees how hard you are working to keep everyone safe. They just see a parent who worries too much.

What you'll come to recognize here is that the watching part of you isn't your enemy, and it isn't broken. It's grief in a different coat. It stood up the day you lost someone and it has been trying, in its anxious, sleepless way, to make sure it never happens again — as if enough watching could have changed anything, as if love could be done carefully enough to be safe. We'll sit with that quiet, awful bargain. We'll look at how the scanning promises protection and delivers exhaustion, how it crowds out the very moments with your kids it claims to be guarding.

We'll talk plainly about the difference between caring for the people at your table and keeping watch over them — between being with them and bracing for the worst — and how grief blurred the line until they felt like the same act of love.

This book won't switch the worry off. It can't, and a book that promised to would be lying to you. There is no quiet that makes you stop loving people who can die. What it offers instead is the other chair: a place to set the worry down for an hour, to be seen by someone who understands why you keep counting, and to remember that you are allowed to be at the table, not just standing beside it.

You can love them without standing guard over them. You're allowed to sit down. Everyone's still here.

Book 2 of the Two Chairs Press grief series.
books.angle: You lost one. Now you can't stop counting the rest.
books.author: Kira Raman
keywords: hypervigilance after death of a loved one
